narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 36 and a middle-income family background, your law school application stood out in some ways but faced challenges in key academic areas that the admissions model scrutinizes closely. The model predicts a 63.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection to protect the school's bar passage rates and reputation. This prediction hinges on your overall profile, particularly your undergraduate GPA of 3.00—slightly below the typical 3.21 average—and your school decile rankings from early years, placing you in the 2nd decile for Year 1 grades (versus an average of 5.71) and the 1st decile for Year 3 (versus 5.53). Among all the factors considered, your Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest pull toward the failure prediction, as being in the bottom of your class that year signaled to the model a notable risk of struggling with the intense demands of law school and the bar. Your LSAT score pushed back in the opposite direction, helping to lower the predicted risk since it's close to the average of 36.7 and shows your aptitude for standardized legal reasoning. Your Year 1 decile also contributed toward the failure outlook, though less forcefully than Year 3, reinforcing concerns about early academic momentum. Compared to typical students who get approved—those often in higher deciles with stronger GPAs—your profile aligns more with past applicants who faced bar hurdles, even as a full-time student like most (92.7% of the dataset). It's understandable to feel frustrated, especially with your LSAT strength and plans to study full-time, but the model prioritizes consistent grade performance as the biggest predictor of bar success. To turn this around, focus on retaking the LSAT if possible to push above average, or strengthen your academic record through a post-bac program or additional coursework to climb those decile rankings. Building a narrative around any upward trends since Year 3, perhaps with letters from professors highlighting your growth, could help in reapplying to schools with more holistic reviews. You're clearly motivated, and with targeted improvements, you can shift your profile toward the successful applicants the model favors. Keep pushing forward—many overcome early setbacks to thrive in law.
